1.synthesis: Before the Passover, knowing his hour had come and his departure to the Father was near, Jesus rises from the supper, lays aside his outer garment, wraps a towel around his waist, and washes his disciples' feet (vv.4–5). Peter refuses; Jesus insists: "If I do not wash you, you have no share with me" (v.8). Peter overcorrects; Jesus distinguishes between the bath that makes one clean and the lesser washing still needed (v.10). After returning to his place, Jesus interprets the act: if he, their Teacher and Lord, has washed their feet, they ought to wash one another's feet (v.14). The servant is not greater than the master; the messenger is not greater than the one who sent him. "Knowing these things, you are blessed if you do them" (v.17). The Psalm 41 citation warns that one among them will fulfill the betrayer's role; the foreknowledge of it, shared before it happens, is evidence for "I am he" (vv.18–19).
1.historical_context: The Fourth Gospel's chronology places this evening before Passover, aligning the crucifixion with the slaughter of the Passover lambs on 14 Nisan afternoon. Guests at a formal Roman-style deipnon reclined on couches around three sides of a low table; the lowest-ranking household slave would wash arriving guests' feet soiled by sandaled travel. Mishnaic tradition explicitly ruled that this task was beneath a disciple's obligations to his teacher — even the shoe-removal the Baptist refused to perform was forbidden (b. Ketubot 96a). Jesus performs what was too low for a disciple, not from ignorance of his status but in full possession of it: "knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands" (v.3). The Greek hyodeigma ("example," v.15) is the Aristotelian term for an exemplar held up for imitation; Jesus grounds ethics in enacted embodiment. The shaliach (agency) principle behind v.20 — a person's authorized representative stands in for the person — is codified in rabbinic literature: "a man's agent is as the man himself" (m. Berakhot 5:5).
1.literary_structure: The footwashing opens the Gospel's second half (sometimes called the Book of Glory) by enacting in miniature what the cross will accomplish: the Lord takes the posture of the slave, serves the community, and gives himself for their cleansing. The layering of garments at v.4 and v.12 — removed, then resumed — echoes the Shepherd's "I lay down my life... and I take it up again" (John 10:17–18). The meros (share/inheritance, v.8) converts the social act into a participation metaphor: receiving service from Christ is the condition of belonging to him. Peter's overcorrection (v.9) and the distinction between bath and foot-washing (v.10) introduce a two-level cleansing that early Christian tradition mapped onto baptism and ongoing repentance. The Psalm 41 citation (v.18) frames the betrayal within the category of the suffering righteous one betrayed by a table companion — the most dishonorable act in the Mediterranean honor code.
1.theological_application: "If I, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another's feet" (v.14) is the most explicit ethical directive in John's Gospel. The structure is qal wa-homer (if the greater, how much more the lesser) applied to servanthood: the one with genuine authority established the pattern; those with derivative authority must enact it. The blessing of v.17 is not given for knowing the principle but for doing it — a consistent Johannine insistence that knowledge without obedience is self-deception. The footwashing teaches that Christian community is constituted not by shared status or honor competition but by mutual deference enacted in concrete, low acts of service. The one who has been washed by Christ is the one who can wash another.
1.christology: "Knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he had come from God and was going back to God" (v.3) is the Christological ground of the footwashing: the act flows from identity, not from self-abnegation. It is precisely because he is Lord that his service means what it means. The Son who holds all authority stoops to perform the slave's task, not temporarily setting aside his divinity but expressing it — this is what divine authority looks like from the inside. The egō eimi of v.19 — "when it does happen you may believe that I am he" — grounds the entire scene in the divine name: the one who washes feet is the one who bears the name above all names, and his knowledge of the betrayal before it happens is evidence of who he is.

21.synthesis: Troubled in spirit, Jesus makes the announcement no table companion could bear to hear: "One of you will betray me" (v.21). The disciples stare at one another, at a loss. Peter signals the Beloved Disciple, who reclines nearest Jesus, to ask who it is. "It is the one to whom I give this morsel when I have dipped it" (v.26) — and he gives it to Judas Iscariot. After receiving the morsel, Satan enters him, and Jesus says: "What you are going to do, do quickly" (v.27). No one at the table understands why. Judas goes out immediately — and it was night (v.30).
21.historical_context: Jesus is "troubled in spirit" (etarachthē tō pneumati) — the verb tarassō used in John for genuine anguish (John 11:33; John 12:27). In the triclinium dining arrangement, the Beloved Disciple reclined immediately to Jesus's right, head near Jesus's chest — the position of highest honor that allowed the private exchange at vv.25–26 to be invisible to the wider group. Dipping bread and handing it to another was a gesture of honor and favor at a formal dinner; Jesus uses this culturally meaningful act as the disclosure mechanism, embedding the revelation of betrayal within the form of honor. The Psalm 41 background already invoked (v.18) — table betrayal as the supreme violation of the companion bond — governs the horror of the morsel's meaning. The entry of Satan (v.27) echoes v.2 (where the devil had "already prompted" Judas) but now marks the decisive moment of possession: the decision becomes irreversible.
21.literary_structure: The scene is structured as a disclosure that illuminates for the reader while remaining opaque to most participants. Only the Beloved Disciple, and through him Peter, know the identity of the betrayer; the rest of the table hears Jesus's command to Judas and misreads it as practical instruction (buy provisions or give alms for the festival, v.29). The two-level structure is characteristic of the Gospel: those closest to Jesus know what others cannot see. "It was night" (v.30) is the Evangelist's most compressed theological notation: factually, it was after dark; theologically, it was the night into which the agent of darkness departs, leaving the light to continue its final discourse unobstructed.
21.theological_application: The betrayal scene carries a moral weight that remains present for every reader of the Gospel: the person who sits at the table with Jesus, receives the gestures of his favor, and goes out into the night is not a figure from a distant past but a standing possibility. Judas received the bread from Jesus's own hand and then departed to sell him. The warning of v.18 — "he who ate my bread has lifted his heel against me" — is not merely a prediction about one man but a testimony to the depth of the estrangement that creaturely freedom can enact even in the presence of infinite love. "Satan entered into him" does not excuse the choice but names its full character: the departure from the supper is the departure from the light.
21.christology: Jesus's foreknowledge of the betrayal, announced before it happens so that it can serve as evidence for "I am he" (v.19), places his control over the events in full view. He does not discover the betrayal; he discloses it. He does not prevent it; he authorizes its timing: "what you are going to do, do quickly" (v.27). The passion, from this moment, proceeds not as something done to Jesus from outside but as something Jesus permits on his own timetable in obedience to the Father. The authority that lays down life and takes it up again (John 10:18) is visible even in the moment of betrayal: the one who is handed over is the one who controls when the handing over begins.

31.synthesis: With Judas departed, Jesus speaks with urgency: "Now the Son of Man is glorified, and God is glorified in him" (v.31). The glorification sequence has begun. He addresses the disciples — "my little children" (v.33), a term unique in the Gospel — with the news of his imminent departure: where he is going, they cannot come now, though they will follow later. Then the new commandment: "Love one another: just as I have loved you, you also are to love one another. By this all people will know that you are my disciples" (vv.34–35). Peter asks where Jesus is going; Jesus's answer defers him: you cannot follow now but will follow afterward. Peter insists he will lay down his life for Jesus. Jesus's counter-prophecy: before the rooster crows, Peter will deny him three times (vv.36–38).
31.historical_context: The "new commandment" (entolē kainē) requires unpacking against Leviticus 19:18's command to love one's neighbor: the command to love is not new; the measure is. "As I have loved you" introduces a Christological calibration — the cross-shaped love of the footwashing and the passion is the standard. The tender address teknia ("little children") appears only here in the Gospel but frequently in 1 John, where it marks the mature teacher's affection for his community. "Where I am going you cannot come" applies the departure formula used against the Jewish leaders (John 7:34; John 8:21) now to the disciples — but with the crucial difference of "for now" and "later you will follow" (v.36). Peter's declaration echoes the Shepherd's language of John 10:11; his genuine courage is real but untested. The rooster crow (third watch, roughly 3 a.m.) marks the time of the denial with precision.
31.literary_structure: The section is a hinge between the betrayal's departure and the farewell discourse. Judas leaving triggers the glorification announcement; the glorification announcement precedes the love command; the love command is the community's mark during the absence that follows. The irony of the closing exchange — Peter declaring readiness to die for Jesus while Jesus predicts his denial — frames the entire farewell discourse within human frailty: the disciples who will receive the Paraclete's coming are the same ones who will scatter, deny, and fail. The love commandment is given not to those who have already achieved it but to those who will need it urgently in the hours and years ahead.
31.theological_application: "By this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you love one another" (v.35) designates mutual love as the one public mark of the Christian community. In a world of competing identity markers — ritual purity, lineage, dietary practice, synagogue membership — Jesus gives his community a single defining practice: the kind of love that his own life exemplifies, poured out without calculation. The new commandment is not a lighter version of the law but a deeper one: it requires not just love of neighbor (who might be defined narrowly) but love of one another (the disciples, in their actual particularity and failure) modeled on the love of the cross. Peter's imminent failure does not disqualify the commandment; it illustrates why the love it requires is qualitatively new.
31.christology: "Now the Son of Man is glorified, and God is glorified in him" (v.31) — the Evangelist presents the departure of the betrayer as the opening of the passion's glorification sequence. In John's theology, the cross is not defeat redeemed by resurrection; it is the definitive act of glorification through which the Father is honored and the Son exalted. The mutual glorification formula — God glorified in Son, Son glorified in God, immediately (v.32) — frames the next twenty-four hours not as catastrophe but as the summit of the Gospel. The love command flows from this Christology: the community that bears the mark of mutual love is the community shaped by the cross, the shape that glorification takes when it is lived rather than proclaimed.
